openingMessage: We win in this business by being invited back the next day. That doesn't just come from doing the job right — it comes from how we carry ourselves as professionals and as people.
leadershipThought: We don't operate exposed. If there's an issue, call it in. Don't argue. Don't escalate. Execute.

lessonsLearned:

• Louie Zarate — Cosmo phase 2 elevated readings — H2S 3,000ppm + CO 3% at 9 AM pull; multi-gas hazard, confirm monitoring plan and supplied air protocol for next crew visit

exxonMoments:
Crane Work — SWA Exercised During Horse Head Install (Line of Fire)
A Canon technician observed a horse head installation using a crane at a Midkiff-area location. During the lift, unsafe conditions were identified and Stop Work Authority was exercised. Atmospheric monitoring, respiratory protection, exclusion zones, and hold points were all active when the stop was called. Crane lifts place every person in the swing radius in the direct line of fire from a suspended load — SWA during the lift is the one control that covers everyone in that zone, not just the operator.

Pre-Lift Plan Before the Hook Was On (Line of Fire)
Before a crane was used at a Stanton-area well, the crew paused to discuss the lift plan, hazards, and ground crew positions before any rigging was attached. A lift plan conversation before the hook is attached establishes who stands where and what happens if the load shifts — that conversation is the line-of-fire control, and it cannot happen after rigging is set.

Hydrovac Hose Burst — SWA, Repair, Full Re-Brief Before Resuming (Line of Fire)
During a job at a Midkiff-area location, a hydrovac hose burst during operations. SWA was exercised immediately, the hose was repaired, and the crew conducted a full job scope re-review with all personnel before resuming. When equipment fails under pressure, the area around it becomes a line-of-fire zone instantly. Fix the equipment and re-brief the crew before anyone steps back in.

Back-to-Back Jobs Don't Get Automatic Repeats (Line of Fire)
A Canon technician completed two consecutive horse head and flow line removals at adjacent locations. All controls confirmed for both. Post-job note: back-to-back jobs of the same type can create complacency — the second job should receive the same hold-point rigor as the first. Horse head removal puts the crew in the swing and drop radius of heavy components. Treating job two as a repeat of job one is how exclusion zones drift.

The Safety Observer Who Rushes Isn't Observing (Line of Fire)
A new Site Safety Escort was coached on-location about LSRA requirements. The key coaching point: the SSE was at risk of matching crew pace rather than maintaining independent oversight. A safety role is not a crew role — the person moving at crew pace is reacting, not watching. You cannot be inside the work zone and observe the line of fire at the same time. Step out. Slow down. Lead from the front.

todaysChallenge: Find one person today and communicate something early — before they need it.
todaysChallengeSubtext: Call leadership. Notify dispatch. Post in Breakroom. No surprises. That is how trust is built.
